text: Thank you very much to the committee for the opportunity to present. My name is Benjamin Flink, and I'm going to be presenting a case from Emory University of a robotic excision of a Eureka remnant with an associated infected cyst. We have no industry relationships to disclose.

Patient is a seventy-four-year-old male who presented with abdominal pain, a right-paramedian abdominal mass that was erythematous. He had fevers, chills, and weakness. A urinary examination revealed a UTI, and blood cultures showed bacteremia. His past medical history was only notable for hypertension. He had no prior abdominal surgeries.

The video displays the external appearance of the abscess and a CT scan showing its extraperitoneal location. Three arrows on the CT scan indicate the top of the bladder, while the intra-abdominal portion corresponding to the urachal cyst is marked between two arrows. A single arrow highlights calcification between the abscess and the urachal cyst inside the abdomen.

Uracal remnants are a result of failure involution of the uracus into the medial umbilical ligament that usually happens as the bladder descends into the pelvis. There are four different types of uracal remnants. First, a patent uracus, pictured in A. Next, a uracal sinus, pictured in B. C, a uracal diverticulum, And last, as seen in this case, a uracal cyst.

Approximately one third of all adults on autopsy studies will have some sort of microscopic remnant, but the frequency of uracal remnants that become clinically apparent is very rare, and the exact incidence is unknown. A very rare condition with a poor prognosis that must always be considered with urechal remnants is urechal carcinoma. The most common presentation in approximately fifty percent of cases is hematuria, which is followed by lower urinary tract symptoms, mucinuria, and least commonly, a mass. The risk of urechal carcinoma is higher in patients who present over the age of fifty-five and have a calcification. These patients specifically require a careful workup and examination for cancer in both the remnant as well as in the bladder.

As a first step, an incision and drainage was first performed to allow the cavity to drain, and the patient was discharged home with a Foley and a week of oral antibiotics with a plan to excise the uracral cyst in the future, similar to the treatment plan of Tazi et al. in their case series of uracral remnants.
The surgeon performs a cystoscopy, visually inspecting the bladder for any signs of a mass or connection related to the uracal cyst, with no abnormalities observed.

In the operating room, the surgical team uses a twelve millimeter camera port in the left mid abdomen and two eight millimeter robotic ports on either side, along with a five millimeter assistant port near Palmer's point, to triangulate on the abscess cavity. Methylene blue is introduced into the bladder to help identify any urinary tract infection or bladder injury. The surgeon carefully dissects the ureacal cyst away from the anterior abdominal wall, removing the peritoneum and fatty tissues surrounding the median umbilical ligament without violating the fascia.

During the dissection, calcified tissue is encountered and set aside for removal later. The surgeon continues dissection along the peritoneum between the anterior abdominal wall and the median umbilical ligament, identifying a soft plane between the urachal cyst and the bladder. Careful dissection frees the urachal cyst from the bladder, achieving hemostasis and avoiding bladder injury.

The surgeon isolates the urachal cyst from the bladder, using bipolar cautery and scissors to ligate it. The median umbilical ligament is dissected free above the bladder level and similarly ligated with bipolar cautery and scissors.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the urachal cyst by removing the remaining tissue connecting it to the abdominal wall. The absence of methylene blue indicates no bladder entry, and the peritoneum is closed with a single layer of interrupted vicryl sutures.
An additional step not pictured in this video was the filling of the abscess cavity with saline to ensure there was no communication of the pneumoperitoneum in the abdomen and the abscess cavity.

The surgeon places the specimen and calcification into a retrieval bag and closes the port sites. The video concludes with the postoperative steps, including a cystogram to confirm bladder integrity and removal of the Foley catheter.

Pathology showed an inflamed uracal remnant with no signs of malignant transformation. Thank you very much for watching our presentation of a robotic excision of a ureco remnant with associated infected cyst.
